Integrated Report of ACS Group 2024
“An integrated

Group building 
the future”

Florentino Pérez
Chairman of the ACS Group
Letter from the Chairman

2024 has been a decisive year for the ACS Group, which 
has consolidated its position as a global leader in the 
infrastructure sector. With a clear strategic focus and 
integrated organisation, we have achieved excellent 
results that reinforce our commitment to innovation 
and transformation in the sectors in which we operate. 
The result is that, through our projects, ACS has driven 
the modernisation of key infrastructure, creating jobs 
and contributing to economic and social progress.

Thanks to the constant efforts of over 157,000 people 
who comprise our Group, we have achieved an 
unprecedented volume of sales: EUR 41,633 million, an 
increase of 16.5% on the previous year. This growth, 
based on our outstanding international presence, has 
positioned us among the leading Spanish companies 
with the greatest global expansion, reflecting the 
leadership of our companies in the main markets.

This progress has also yielded outstanding operating 
results, with EBITDA up 28.7% to EUR 2,456 million and 
net attributable profit of EUR 828 million, 6.1% higher 
than in 2023. These data are not just figures; they reflect 
a strategy that combines investment, sustainability and 
profitability, as set out in our Strategic Plan 2024-2026.

In particular, the total return for our shareholders in 
2024 exceeded 26.9%, as a result of dividends paid 
during the year and a 20.6% annual revaluation in our 
share price. Over the last five years, the average annual 
return has been 14.1%, while over the last 25 years we 
have achieved an average return of 12.7%.

We are confident that this positive trend will continue. 
The context is favourable because the demand for 
traditional and new generation infrastructure - linked to 
sectors such as digitalisation, mobility or new energy 
sources - continues to grow.

We are very well positioned, as evidenced by the fact 
that our total project backlog reached EUR 88,209 
million, up 19.9% compared to 2023.

This success is also built on strong alliances with the 
best partners. In 2024, we have strengthened our 
shareholder base with the incorporation of CriteriaCaixa, 
an international benchmark, and the subsequent 
appointment of its chairman, Isidro Fainé, as vice-
chairman of our Group.

But 
beyond 
economic 
achievements, 
we 
have 
experienced moments of deep reflection and solidarity. 
We were deeply shaken by the havoc wreaked by the 
DANA floods in Valencia, a disaster that devastated the 
lives of the Valencian people and touch all Spaniards. 
In the face of adversity, we responded with solidarity 
and, through our Foundation and various Group 
companies, we made ourselves available to the public 
administrations to work in the rebuilding of damaged 
infrastructure and the reactivation of the affected 
areas. This is the spirit that defines ACS: a commitment 
to society that transcends mere business activity.

This year we also suffered the sad loss of Javier 
Echenique, a director of the company, whose 20 years 
of dedication to the growth and expansion of the Group 
have left an indelible mark on us all. His legacy lives on 
in our culture and values, which guide our every step.

We enter 2025 with the same enthusiasm and 
commitment that have brought us this far and that will 
continue to be key to the evolution of our company. We 
will continue to work to ensure that shareholders, 
customers, employees, institutions and suppliers 
continue to trust in the future of our Group.

Letter from the CEO

Growth, integration and future reflect the evolution of 
the ACS Group in 2024, a year in which we have 
continued to strengthen the foundations of a cohesive, 
expanding project committed to profitability.

In the year, we achieved a net profit of EUR 828 million 
and our sales increased by 16.5% to over EUR 41,600 
million. In addition, our project backlog has grown by 
20% year-on-year and reached an record high: EUR 
88,209 million. Approximately 60% of this figure comes 
from the United States, where we are established leader 
in the sector.

By business area, the companies specialising in 
Integrated Solutions contributed EUR 536 million to the 
Group's results, while the Engineering and Construction 
and Infrastructure segments contributed EUR 156 
million and EUR 205 million, respectively.

Similarly, in 2024, the Group has generated a net 
operating cash flow of almost EUR 2,100 million, an 
increase of over EUR 1,000 million compared with the 
previous year. This is undoubtedly one of the most 
important indicators because it measures our ability to 
invest, grow and remunerate our shareholders.

The data shows that we continue to make progress on 
the targets set out in our Strategic Plan 2024-2026, 
presented last April, in which we set ourselves the goal 
of generating up to EUR 4,000 million net operating 
cash flows and up to EUR 1,000 million of net profit by 
2026. A solid roadmap to boost our growth through 
three key pillars: ACS's global leadership in high-growth 
and high added value segments; the reduction of the 
Group's risk profile; and the boost to investment in 
infrastructure, both traditional and new generation.

In 2024 we have made great strides in this direction. For 
example, the awards made to the Group in high-growth 
segments such as digitalisation, mobility, biopharma 
and critical metals, among others, already account for 
50% of the total. In addition, we have continued to push 
for contracts with a low risk profile, which represent 
more than 85% of our current backlog.

| P. 6
Integrated Report of ACS Group 2024
“Investment

is the driver for 
our growth and 
profitability”

Juan Santamaría 
CEO of the ACS Group

Investment, the driver for our future growth and 
profitability, has established itself as one of our 
greatest strengths. In this line, our strategy for the 
development of data centres stands out, which has 
made it possible for us to already have a pipeline of 
2.1GW of projects under development. In addition, we 
have identified opportunities for an additional 4GW in 
key regions in Europe, Australia and the United States.

At the same time, we have taken steps in the natural 
resources sector, where CIMIC has acquired an 
additional 10% holding in Thiess, in which it now holds 
60% of the capital, giving it effective control of its 
operations and investment policy.

In the area of traditional infrastructure, we have also 
made significant progress, such as the award to a 
consortium led by Iridium, our concession development 
subsidiary, of a project to finance, build and operate for 
50 years more than 25 km of managed lanes on the 
SR400 highway in Atlanta (Georgia).

Meanwhile, Abertis has continued to grow in the 
transport concessions operating segment, increasing 
its sales by 9.8% in 2024 to over EUR 6,000 million. The 
company plans to generate more than EUR 27,000 
million in cash by 2033, which will allow it to maintain 
its current dividend policy over the next decade and 
continue to invest in new assets. This commitment to 
growth is evidenced, for example, by transactions such 
as the recent acquisition of a majority holding in the 
French A-63 toll motorway, which we closed in early 
2025.

To supplement these strategic levers, we have 
continued to focus on adding specialised engineering 
and systems capabilities. This includes the acquisitions 
of 
engineering 
and 
mining 
services 
companies 
announced by Turner, Sedgman, Thiess and CIMIC.

We have also enhanced our operational efficiency 
through 
strategic 
integration 
and 
resource 
rationalisation. An example of progress in this area is 
the merger between Flatiron and Dragados North 
America, which led to the creation of the second largest 
civil engineering contractor in the United States.

The high cash flow generated by the ACS Group and its 
solid financial position, with a net debt of less than 0.3 
times the Group's consolidated EBITDA, provide us with 
a capacity of more than EUR 6,000 million until 2030, 
i.e. around EUR 1,000 million per year without 
increasing our debt, to undertake future investments 
that will allow us to capitalise on the opportunities 
offered by the market.

And we plan to do so while intending to maintain 
attractive returns for shareholders. In line with this, the 
Board of Directors has proposed an increase in 
shareholder remuneration against the 2024 results, 
which is a clear demonstration of our commitment to 
value creation and the strength of our business model.

We are aware that all this is only possible thanks to the 
talent of our professionals, who have become ACS' main 
competitive edge. That is why this year we have gone a 
step further by launching ACS University, an educational 
platform that draws on the experience of our companies 
and 
with 
which 
we 
want 
to 
offer 
employees 
opportunities to develop new skills and continue to grow 
professionally. Enhancing their specialisation and training 
is particularly important in the context of the sophistication 
of the markets in which we operate.

This talent will also be key to ensuring the sustainability 
of our processes and products. We continue to take 
steps in this direction, as evidenced by our ambitious 
environmental targets, which include achieving climate 
neutrality by 2045.  Acknowledgements such as the ACS 
Group's inclusion in the Dow Jones Sustainability Index 
(DJSI) in 2024 reinforce our conviction that this is the 
way forward.

We know that our global presence, our offer of high 
added value projects and the talent we have place us in 
a magnificent position to continue contributing value to 
our different stakeholders. This value involves, among 
other things, continuing to grow and increase the size 
of ACS. That is my goal and my challenge as CEO of a 
Group that has increased its market capitalisation 
twofold in the last three years, and whose ambition is 
to continue contributing to building a better future.

| 2.1. Who we are

The ACS Group is a global infrastructure operator and 
provider 
comprising 
leading 
companies 
in 
the 
development, building and operation of engineering, 
construction and infrastructure development projects in 
the transportation, environment, building, digital, natural 
resources and advanced technology segments.

The ACS Group is formed of over 157,000 employees 
who work in an integrated manner in the three most 
highly developed markets in the world (North America, 
Asia-Pacific and Europe), generating synergies and 
ensuring that ACS is present along the entire value 
chain of the infrastructure business.

With sales of more than EUR 41,600 million in 2024 and 
order backlog of some EUR 88,200 million at year-end, 
the Group's international track record has once again

been recognised by the prestigious Engineering News-
Record (ENR) magazine. Specifically, ACS is a leader in 
the data centre, biopharma, chemical and mining 
sectors, as well as in the rail infrastructure, motorways, 
airports, bridges and sports and commercial facilities 
segment. Also in the concessions segment, as a 
greenfield assets developer and brownfield infrastructure 
operator.

With an approach based on adapting to the various 
markets and each project’s needs, the Group’s 
companies have a common corporate culture and 
values geared towards excellence, added value 
creation, profit generation and innovation, which 
contributes to the advancement of the communities in 
which it operates.
Areas of activity

The ACS Group's global presence, together with its 
positioning in new-generation sectors and its vast 
knowledge of engineering, civil engineering and 
building, places the Group in an extraordinary position

to embrace the opportunities offered by the market and 
transform the future of infrastructure.

| 2.2. Opportunities and challenges

Digitalisation, demographics, 
deglobalisation and energy demand

The design, construction and modernisation of infrastructure 
continues to emerge as a pillar of socio-economic 
development and remains high on the agenda of 
governments, businesses and investors.

Phenomena 
such 
as 
digitalisation, 
demographic 
changes or new energy demand offer opportunities for 
the development of new infrastructure that connects 
people, contributes to create new city models that meet 
citizens' needs and, ultimately, facilitate progress.

Demand for high added value infrastructure also 
continues to grow due to the de-globalisation or 
relocation of key industries in the world's main 
developed markets, mainly in strategic sectors such as 
pharmaceuticals, healthcare, manufacturing, technology, 
defence and aviation, among others.

In the field of digitalisation, opportunities lie in areas 
such 
as 
the 
creation 
of 
solutions 
for 
digital 
transformation, the deployment of telecommunications 
networks 
and 
the 
creation 
of 
semiconductor 
manufacturing facilities. Also key is the development of 
data centres, essential infrastructure to enable the 
storage, processing and management of large volumes 
of information.

Specifically, in the data centre sector, demand is 
expected to exceed 139 GW globally by 2028, driven by 
generative artificial intelligence (AI) and the migration 
of information to the cloud from traditional storage 
sources. AI will not only drive this segment but will also 
serve as a tool to leverage the large volumes of 
information available when making project life-cycle 
decisions.

The sectors of sustainable mobility, transportation and 
energy demand will also attract significant investment 
in the coming years, driven by the need to move 
towards production models with less impact on the 
environment and more liveable cities. In these areas, 
the development of advanced energy storage systems, 
the creation of battery recycling plants, innovation in 
new technologies such as hydrogen, the electrification 
of transportation and the creation and deployment of 
smart urban mobility systems, among others, are 
important.

In the building sector, there are already opportunities 
linked to industrialised construction, which offers 
advantages such as shorter build times, lower costs, 
use of more sustainable materials and a reduction in 
the volume of waste generated.

The ACS Group has evolved from a company specialising in construction to an operator and provider of 
infrastructure worldwide. The Group comprises leading companies in developing, constructing and operating 
engineering, civil engineering and building projects and new-generation solutions in sectors of the future such as 
digitalisation, smart mobility and energy demand.

ACS carries out its operations through companies with a cross-cutting activity that cover different sectors, allowing 
it to create synergies between them and offer a complete service to its customers.
I. Integrated solutions

In line with the Strategic Plan 2024-2026, one of the ACS Group's main pillars is the promotion of new-generation 
infrastructure projects in all its markets.

Through the companies Turner and CIMIC, the ACS Group designs, builds and operates integral projects with high 
added value: from technological solutions such as data centres to telecommunications networks, as well as 
facilities for the production of semiconductors, solutions for the defence, health, biopharma and sports industries, 
and projects related to energy demand such as renewable assets, battery factories, power transmission lines and 
energy storage systems.

These segments are in high demand as a result of technological advances, new energy models and demographic 
changes. In fact, 50% of the ACS Group's new contracts awarded in 2024 were in this business area.

knowledge, methodologies, ideas and 
good practices.
This philosophy is part of the Group's 
commitment to fostering innovation to 
give life to projects that have a positive 
impact on society and act as a catalyst for
progress.

The ACS Group offers its shareholders 
sustainable growth through investment in 
new-generation infrastructure and through 
the development of engineering projects 
linked to traditional sectors such as 
transportation.  In line with its Strategic 
Plan 2024-2026, ACS combines its 
commitment to growth with attractive 
shareholder remuneration.

ACS Group companies build and operate 
infrastructure that connects people, while 
developing key technological solutions to 
enable advances in a 'hyperconnected' 
world. In parallel to promoting innovation, 
ACS companies encourage the 
implementation of sustainable and 
environmentally responsible practices, 
while at the same time contributing to job 
creation.

The work of ACS Group companies 
contributes to transforming customers’ 
activities in strategic sectors. By 
combining its experience in development 
of new-generation assets with cutting-
edge skills in civil engineering and 
conventional infrastructure, the Group is 
able to effectively meet the needs of its 
customers, with whom it shares the risks 
and benefits associated with projects.
A cohesive Group, leader and global benchmark

“One Group, One Team”. This summarises the evolution 
of the ACS Group throughout its history. Our picture 
today presents us with the image of a world leader and 
benchmark formed by a group of integrated companies 
integrated which share a same strategy.

Thanks 
to 
efficient 
organisation 
and 
flexible 
management, the ACS Group companies have a 
cohesive strategic plan, aimed at creating synergies, 
meeting the needs of our customers and maximize the 
profitability of our shareholders.

| 2.8. The ACS Group’s governance model

The ACS Group's governance model is based on ACS's 
corporate values as established in its Code of Conduct: 
integrity, excellence, trust. These values, which serve as 
the basis for the culture of ethics and regulatory 
compliance that has governed the Group's activities 
since its inception, inspire the set of rules, policies and 
procedures that make up the ACS Governance System, 
which includes the regulation of the composition, 
structure, competencies and functioning of the 
Company's governing bodies, as well as the relations 
between these bodies and their members and 
executives.

Based on these values, the regulations that make up 
the ACS Governance System are articulated and are 
essentially set out in:
• The 
Company 
By-laws, 
which 
establish 
the 
fundamental 
rules 
and 
principles 
guiding 
the 
organisation and functioning of the Company’s 
management bodies, and are implemented through 
the Shareholders’ General Meeting By-laws and the 
Rules of the Board of Directors.
• The ACS Group's Code of Conduct, the supreme rule,

which presides over the organisation's entire body of 
regulations, with application for the entire the ACS 
Group.
• The corporate policies aimed at establishing the

principles and criteria that must serve as a basis for 
the governance model of the Company and its Group 
and how they conduct their activities.
• The Compliance and Risk policies and procedures

that make up the Global Compliance Management 
System and the Comprehensive Risk Control and 
Management System, which, respecting the Group's 
highly decentralised management structure, enable 
ACS, as the Group's parent company, to define, 
evaluate and supervise the different areas of 
Compliance, as well as the financial and non-financial 
risk control and management systems at Group level, 
including strategic and geopolitical, operational,

General Shareholders' Meeting

The ACS Group's General Shareholders' Meeting, as the 
supreme body expressing the will of the Company, is 
the main channel for shareholder engagement in the 
Company. Its decisions are taken in accordance with 
applicable laws and the Company By-laws and the 
Shareholders’ General Meeting By-laws and are binding 
on all shareholders.

The General Shareholders' Meeting is responsible, 
among other functions, for approving the annual 
accounts, the management report, which shall include 
the mandatory information on sustainability, the 
distribution of profits, the approval of corporate 
management and the appointment and removal of 
directors, as well as any other functions attributed to it 
by law or the ACS internal regulations. The Company is 
responsible for ensuring equal treatment of all 
shareholders who are in the same position with regard 
to the rights to information, attendance, participation 
and the exercise of voting rights at the General 
Meeting.

Board of Directors

The ACS Board of Directors represents the Company, 
conducts 
its 
businesses 
and 
carries 
out 
any 
transactions that constitute its purpose or are related 
to it, in addition to its non-delegable functions in 
accordance with the Law and the By-Laws.

Without prejudice to the foregoing, the policy of the 
Board of Directors is to focus its activity on the general 
function of defining the strategic and management 
guidelines for the Company, taking into account the 
projection of the same on ACS Group companies, 
entrusting the administrative and management bodies 
of the companies forming part of the Group with the 
functions of day-to-day management and effective 
management thereof, without prejudice to establishing 
the necessary mechanisms for the exchange of 
information and coordination, also ensuring that the 
corporate interest of the Company is reconciled with 
that of these entities.

In the exercise of its duties, the Board of Directors acts 
with unity of purpose and independence of judgement, 
treating all shareholders in the same position equally, 
and is guided by corporate interest, understood as the 
achievement of a profitable and sustainable business 
in the long term, thus creating value in the Company 
and its Group, also taking into consideration the other 
stakeholders that may be affected by its activity.

As of the date of this Report, the ACS Board of 
Directors is made up of 14 members of recognised 
prestige and professional competence who act with 
independent judgement in the performance of their 
duties, having a diversity of skills, experience, training 
and gender, among other aspects, thus ensuring overall 
suitability for the best performance by the Board and its 
Committees of their respective duties. The main 
changes in its composition in 2024 were the 
incorporation of Mr Isidro Fainé as proprietary director 
appointed at the proposal of the significant shareholder 
Criteria Caixa, S.A.U., a director who is also chairman of 
the aforementioned significant shareholder and of the 
"La Caixa" Foundation; the voluntary resignation of the 
independent director Mr Antonio Botella; and the death 
of the proprietary director Mr Javier Echenique, who 
devoted 20 years to the promotion and expansion of 
the ACS Group.

In line with the spirit of continuous improvement in 
corporate 
governance, 
the 
Board 
of 
Directors 
undergoes an annual evaluation of its operations, as 
well as that of its Committees, in order to identify areas 
for improvement and develop action plans for their 
implementation and monitoring.

Board Committees

To make its functions more effective, the Board has the 
following committees, the members of which are 
appointed taking into account the knowledge, skills and 
experience necessary for the optimum performance of 
their duties and in accordance with applicable 
regulations.
• Executive Committee: Delegated Committee of the

Board of Directors that exercises, by delegation of the 
Board of Directors, all powers that correspond to the 
Board except those that are legally or statutorily non-
delegable.
• Audit and Sustainability Committee: Internal Board

Committee that is attributed, in accordance with the 
legal provisions and with the recommendations of 
good governance, functions relating to:

◦Oversee 
financial 
and 
non-financial 
reporting, 
internal 
control 
and 
internal 
audit 
and 
risk 
management and control.
◦Maintain relations with the external auditor and the

auditor for sustainability information, and submit to 
the Board proposals for the selection, appointment, 
re-election and replacement thereof, preserving their 
independence in the exercise of their functions.
◦Oversee the Company's Governance System, which

includes ACS's policies, rules and internal codes of 
conduct, among others, in the areas of compliance, 
corporate governance, sustainability, information 
security and privacy, data protection, risks and 
internal management and control systems.
◦Report on related-party transactions that must be

approved by the General Meeting or the Board and 
supervise the internal reporting and periodic checks 
procedure established by the Company for those 
transactions for which approval has been delegated 
by the Board in accordance with the applicable 
regulations.
◦Reporting to the Board on all matters envisaged in

the law, the Company By-Laws and the Rules of the 
Board of Directors.
• Appointments Committee:  Internal Board Committee
that is attributed, in accordance with the legal

provisions and with the recommendations of good 
governance, functions relating to:
◦The composition of the Board.
◦The selection and appointment of directors and
senior management.
◦The positions and structure of the Board.
◦The annual evaluation of the Board and its
committees.

◦Design 
and 
structuring 
of 
regular 
refresher 
programmes for directors.
• Remuneration Committee:  Internal Board Committee

that is attributed, in accordance with the legal 
provisions and with the recommendations of good 
governance, functions relating to:
◦Proposing to the Board the remuneration policy for

directors and senior executives and reviewing it 
periodically.
◦Proposing the contractual conditions of executive

directors and senior executives’ contracts in terms 
of remuneration.
◦Reporting to the Board on the setting of the
individual remuneration of each director.
◦Reporting on the proposal of multi-year plans that
may be established.
◦Verifying the information on directors' and senior

executives’ remuneration contained in corporate 
documents.
◦To ensure that potential conflicts of interest do not

adversely affect the independence of external 
advice provided to the Committee.

the Ordinary General Shareholders' Meeting.

On 16 January 2025, ACS, Actividades de Construcción 
y Servicios, S.A. agreed to carry out the second capital 
increase charged to reserves approved by the

Meeting. Shareholders could choose between receiving 
cash (at EUR 0.454/share) or new shares. 41.01% of 
the free allotment rights were sold to ACS, resulting in a 
dividend payment of EUR 50,582,972.96 on 6 February 
2025. Finally, 1,378,681 new shares were issued with a 
simultaneous 
increase 
and 
redemption 
of 
EUR 
689,340.50, with no impact on the total number of 
shares outstanding.

In addition, on 7 January 2025, Turner Corporation 
completed the acquisition of Dornan Engineering for 
EUR 410 million in cash. Dornan, which specialises in 
engineering for high-tech projects in Europe, will be 
consolidated in ACS's financial statements in 2025

under IFRS 3. The value of assets, liabilities, goodwill 
and intangibles is not yet available. The goodwill is 
attributed to Dornan's experience and growth potential 
in 
the 
European 
technology 
sector. 
With 
this 
acquisition, Turner seeks to accelerate its expansion in 
Europe, offering turnkey solutions with its low-risk 
construction 
business 
model 
in 
the 
high-end 
technology market.

Lastly, the talks held in 2024 resulted in an agreement 
announced on 28 February 2025, whereby Abertis will 
acquire a 51.2% interest in the A-63 highway in France, 
a strategic corridor between Spain and northern 
Europe. The transaction strengthens the Group’s 
backlog and financial soundness and contributes to its 
growth and cash flow replacement strategy. The 
transaction will be concluded subject to regulatory 
approvals.

| 4.1. Sustainability strategy

ACS is a global leader in the development of 
infrastructures, key sectors for society with the aim of 
building a better future. Its commitment to the social 
and economic progress of the countries in which it 
operates is realised through the development, financing 
and 
operation 
of 
engineering, 
civil 
works 
and 
construction projects, as well as new generation 
solutions in high added value sectors such as energy 
transition, digitalisation and smart mobility. The 
promotion of sustainable development is one of the 
fundamental pillars of its corporate strategy with the 
aim 
of 
generating 
shared 
value 
for 
all 
ACS 
stakeholders.

Thus, in the area of Sustainability, the ACS Group's 
strategy is based on a series of common objectives

developed in the 2025 Sustainability Master Plan 
approved by the Board of Directors in 2021 and on 
those defined in the Group's Sustainability Policy. The 
different companies, under these common principles 
and with autonomy in their areas of competence, 
manage their resources as efficiently as possible in 
order to contribute to the achievement of the 
objectives.

This 2025 Sustainability Master Plan sets out the 
Group’s three strategic priorities and the twelve 
commitments with regard to sustainability that the ACS 
Group has set itself to continue driving the overall 
sustainability in infrastructure:

These three commitments include 26 strategic lines 
and 38 objectives for 2025 with the aim of continuing 
to create shared value and maintaining the ACS 
Group’s position as a global leader in the infrastructure 
sector.

Considering the requirements of stakeholders in terms 
of sustainability and the material issues identified, the

ACS Group has established 17 of these objectives as 
priorities for 2025. These objectives are established at 
Group level based on the business model of each of the 
companies that make up the Group, as well as ACS's 
sustainability strategy.

| 4.2. Double materiality analysis

In 2024, the ACS Group carried out an exhaustive 
Double Materiality Analysis in order to identify and 
understand in depth its impact on the environment and 
the external factors that may influence its performance. 
Although this analysis responds to the requirements of 
the 
European 
Corporate 
Sustainability 
Reporting 
Directive (CSRD), ACS has gone beyond mere 
regulatory compliance, turning it into a strategic 
opportunity to continue integrating sustainability into 
the Group's DNA.

This process has been developed with the collaboration 
of all the Group's companies and the active 
participation of the heads of the specialised areas, 
ensuring an integrated approach aligned with the ACS 
Group's business model. In addition, the opinion of the 
main stakeholders has been taken into account, 
allowing us to understand their expectations and needs 
in order to strengthen the relationship with them and 
respond to their concerns.

The analysis has been carried out in full alignment with 
the standards of the European directive, ensuring a

rigorous approach in line with international best 
practice.
A key exercise in sustainability leadership

The main objective of this analysis is to carry out an 
exhaustive assessment of all ACS Group companies in 
order to identify the Impacts, Risks and Opportunities 
and to determine the material issues to be developed in 
the sustainability report, from a dual perspective:
• Financial materiality: analyses how the environment

influences the development of the ACS Group, its 
financial position and performance, cash flows, 
access to financing and cost of capital.
• Impact materiality:  analyses how the ACS Group's

different activities affect the environment and people 
in the short, medium or long term, both positively and 
negatively.

This analysis facilitates the integration of material 
sustainability issues as a key element of the ACS 
Group's strategy and decision-making.

associated impact. However, this impact is not only 
seen as a challenge, but also as an opportunity to 
generate shared value and move towards a more 
sustainable model. Likewise, due to the climate 
emergency, the ACS Group aims to contribute to the 
transition to a low carbon economy by promoting 
products and services that have a smaller impact on

processes in its activities.

As a result of these commitments, the company has 
defined an environmental management framework 
comprising the Group's Environmental Policy, approved 
by the Board on 14 November 2018 and updated on 17 
December 2024, which is articulated by the different 
management systems implemented in the Group

accommodate

and

ACS Group 
Environmental Policy

With this vision in mind, the Group works in several key 
areas that make up its environmental strategy, as set 
out below:

ACS does not see environmental challenges as isolated 
issues, but as an interconnected set of challenges and 
opportunities. Action in one area generates impacts in 
others, and it is precisely in this synergy that the 
Group's true capacity for transformation lies. Each 
measure taken not only protects the environment, but 
also creates value for people and society as a whole.
Climate change

As a global company, the ACS Group is aware of the 
important role it can play in the fight against climate 
change. Therefore, the ACS Group has established, as 
part of the objectives of its Sustainability Master Plan, 
the promotion of energy efficiency and the reduction of 
emissions in its business activities, with the objective 
of achieving climate neutrality (Net Zero) in 2045.

To this end, in 2024, the ACS Group developed a first 
ACS Group Climate Change Mitigation Transition Plan 
(CCCTMP), which will continue to be updated, 
completed and strengthened throughout 2025 and 
beyond.

| 5.1. Efficient Management of Risks and Opportunities

The ACS Group considers it essential to have an 
efficient risk management policy that guarantees the 
continuity and success of its operations, adequately 
protects its investments, ensures compliance with its 
sustainability commitments, and includes policies that 
support the correct execution of regulations and 
safeguard the integrity of its information systems. This 
ensures the Group's continued leadership in the 
industry and the achievement of its objectives, in line 
with the Strategic Plan presented in 2024.

In the ACS Group, risk and opportunity management is 
an integral process based on the Integrated Control and 
Management System, defined in the Risk Control and 
Management Policy. The management structure is 
organised into specific Areas of Responsibility, which 
are 
coordinated 
through 
the 
Governance 
and 
Compliance Committee. This committee ensures the 
representation of all areas involved, guaranteeing an 
aligned and coherent management.

Following the latest update of the Risk Map, the Group 
has identified geopolitical risks, risks related to its 
investments, operational risks (bid and contract 
management), Compliance, cybersecurity and talent 
attraction and retention risks as the most relevant 
risks. For all of them, the Group has defined policies 
and strategies to ensure their adequate mitigation and 
control.

In the area of sustainability, the Group has conducted a 
double materiality assessment in accordance with the 
new 
Corporate 
Sustainability 
Reporting 
Directive 
(CSRD) criteria and EFRAG's Double Materiality 
implementation guide. This involves assessing how the 
environment affects the company economically and

reputationally (financial materiality) and how its 
activities affect the environment positively or negatively 
(impact materiality). This dual approach allows the ACS 
Group to adopt a detailed understanding of the Group's 
status in terms of sustainability, as well as a deeper 
and more comprehensive look at its responsibility and 
role in the economic, social and environmental 
environment.

The Group also understands the importance of 
managing emerging risks. These include geopolitical 
risks such as supply chain disruption and shortages of 
building materials, as well as water scarcity and 
biodiversity loss. In addition, the group has equipped 
itself with tools to assess climate change risk, the 
management of which is critical to the sustainability of 
its operations. Managing these emerging risks is a 
long-term 
strategy 
that 
will 
enable 
continuous 
adaptation to a constantly changing environment.

A crucial aspect of the Group's management is the 
identification 
of 
opportunities. 
The 
Group's 
comprehensive perspective strategically positions it to 
capitalise on them, strengthening its competitiveness 
and 
ensuring 
long-term 
sustainability. 
On 
the 
geopolitical front, the Group is prepared to respond to 
defence and reconstruction needs in various regions. It 
is 
also 
committed 
to 
energy 
generation, 
the 
development of advanced technological infrastructure 
and improving the resilience of the built environment. In 
addition, major opportunities are identified in the 
mining of lithium, critical minerals and rare earths, 
which are essential for the above technologies.

| 5.2. General Risk Control and Management Policy

The ACS Group faces multiple intrinsic risks due to the 
different countries, activities and markets in which it 
operates and the tasks it performs, which could 
potentially impair or even prevent it from achieving its 
objectives and carrying out its strategies successfully. 
The 
ACS 
Group 
considers 
Risk 
Control 
and 
Management to be one of the essential pillars of its 
long-term value creation strategy. Therefore, one of the 
Group's strategic priorities is to improve and strengthen 
this management to increase its effectiveness.

The Group's General Risk Control and Management 
Policy contains the definition of the Comprehensive 
Risk Control and Management System. This Policy 
ratifies ACS's commitment, covering the companies in 
the group of which ACS is the parent, to the 
development of the necessary capacities so that risks 
of all kinds are adequately identified, measured, 
prioritised, managed and controlled. In addition to the 
regulatory framework established by the Policy, the 
different Group companies may develop their own 
within the framework of their respective functional 
spheres and areas of responsibility, in accordance with 
their characteristics and the regulatory framework 
applicable in each case, respecting in all cases the 
principles and framework regulations of ACS's General 
Risk Control and Management Policy.

In the update of the Policy approved in December 2024, 
the role of an ACS Group Chief Risk Officer has been 
included, who reports, in line with the Organisation's 
corporate governance model, to both the CEO and the

Audit Committee. Among other duties, the Chief Risk 
Officer is responsible for promoting the dissemination 
of the ACS Group's risk management culture within all 
of its subsidiaries and for overseeing its consistent, 
structured application, in accordance with the Group's 
Risk Control and Management Policy. All of this is done 
in coordination with the corresponding areas of 
responsibility (Sustainability, Compliance, Finance, Tax, 
Cybersecurity and Data Protection). Under the basic 
principles of the Risk Control and Management Policy, 
the Chief Risk Officer’s role is functionally and 
hierarchically independent from the lines of business.

Dual System for risk monitoring 
and control

The ACS Group is structured in accordance with a 
decentralised management model and carries out its 
activity through a large group of companies, which 
share ACS's culture and values, as well as working 
synergies between them, although each operates 
independently in its respective functional spheres and 
areas of responsibility.

In this respect, the heads of each division and Group 
companies, 
through 
the 
corresponding 
risk 
management 
departments, 
are 
assigned 
the 
development of the appropriate risk control and 
management systems and the necessary internal 
regulations to ensure their implementation and 
operation, consistent with the Group's guidelines.

Areas of responsibility

Once the risk categories have been defined, it is 
essential to establish the areas of responsibility for risk 
management, ensuring that each group within the 
organisation is clear about their respective risk 
management obligations. These areas of responsibility 
are defined in the relevant Group policies, which in turn 
define 
the 
applicable 
control 
and 
management 
methodology in accordance with the common criteria 
set out in the Comprehensive Risk Control and 
Management System.
Risk identification and assessment

The ACS Group's risk methodology envisages the 
identification of the significant risks to which the Group 
companies may be exposed in each of the areas of 
responsibility. Risk identification is a systematic 
process by which potential events or scenarios that 
may have an adverse impact on the achievement of the 
organisation's objectives are recognised and defined. 
This process is a critical step in risk management, as it 
makes it possible to anticipate and prepare proactive 
responses to various threats that may arise.

During risk identification, information is gathered from 
a variety of sources, both internal and external, and a 
variety 
of 
techniques 
are 
used 
to 
ensure 
a 
comprehensive 
view 
of 
potential 
risks. 
These 
techniques vary based on the intrinsic characteristics 
of the different types of risks.

Once the risks have been identified, their likelihood of 
occurrence and potential impact, both financial and 
reputational, are assessed on a uniform scale for all 
risks. The assessment enables the organisation to 
develop appropriate strategies to manage risks 
effectively.

As 
with 
identification, 
the 
different 
areas 
of 
responsibility will use the appropriate tools to 
determine the likelihood of occurrence and impact 
based on the intrinsic characteristics of the risks 
assessed by each of them. This is done by collecting 
data from external and internal sources, using historical 
data, statistics and expert judgements.

Qualitative scales (e.g. very unlikely, unlikely, probable, 
very likely) or more precise quantitative analyses may 
be used. In the impact assessment, financial and 
reputational aspects, among others, are taken into 
consideration. As in the likelihood analysis, the impact 
can be assessed qualitatively (e.g. very low, low, 
medium, high and very high) or quantitatively through 
more precise analyses depending on the risk type.

Level of risk that the Group considers 
acceptable

The ACS Board is aware of the importance of adequate 
control and management of the risks that affect the 
achievement of its strategic and operational objectives, 
and for this reason the ACS Group's General Risk 
Control and Management Policy aims to achieve a 
moderate risk profile through prudent management. 
ACS's business model corresponds to that of a 
decentralised group diversified by geographic areas, 
types of assets, portfolios and customers, with a high 
international presence, mainly in developed countries, 
maintaining a medium/low risk profile in each of them, 
and seeking sustainable long-term growth, protecting 
the organisation's assets and reputation. This model 
must 
take 
into 
account 
the 
expectations 
of 
stakeholders.

ACS's 
different 
areas 
of 
responsibility 
will 
be 
responsible for proposing specific quantitative and 
qualitative limits for the different risk categories, which 
will be approved by the Board under the supervision of 
the Risk Department. They also ensure that these limits 
are documented and clearly communicated throughout 
the organisation.

The level of risk that the Group considers acceptable 
will always be calculated in relation to the level of 
residual risk, i.e. in relation to the risk that remains 
considering the implementation of actions and controls 
for the mitigation of the initial level of risk or the risk 
inherent in the performance of an activity.

For example, at the proposal of the Risk Department, 
the ACS Group has determined that certain Design and 
Construction 
turnkey 
contracts 
are 
initially 
incompatible with its risk appetite. Therefore, the areas 
responsible 
for 
business 
development 
and 
risk 
management in tenders have been informed of the 
need to consult with the Risk Department before 
deciding to participate in these types of projects.

Control and mitigation mechanisms

Risk control and mitigation mechanisms are the 
strategies and actions that are implemented to reduce 
the likelihood of risks occurring and/or to minimise 
their impact if they materialise, in cases where 
acceptance of the risk is not possible. The controls can 
be of varying nature: preventive, detective or corrective, 
again varying based on the specific risk management 
area concerned. In addition to controls, impact or 
probability mitigation strategies and actions include 
transferring the risk to a third party, e.g. subcontractors 
or insurance companies, or simply not accepting the 
risk.

ACS's 
different 
areas 
of 
responsibility 
will 
be 
responsible for defining and proposing the controls and

Risk Map

The Risk Map of ACS and the Group companies is the 
document within the Comprehensive Risk Control and 
Management System that provides a comprehensive 
view of the different risks to which the Group is 
exposed. The objective of the Map is to inform all 
members of the organisation about the main risks 
affecting the Group and to serve as a guide for 
monitoring the events associated with these risks, as 
well as the control and mitigation measures that are in 
place or will be implemented in the future.

For the preparation of the Risk Map, the different areas 
of responsibility of the Group's companies share with 
ACS Risk Department their vision and assessment of 
the risks they identify within the scope of their 
responsibility. Based on this information, the Map 
identifies the main classes of risk that could have an 
impact on the Group's activities and assesses their 
potential impact and likelihood.

The ACS Group's Risk Map analyses risks by grouping 
them into classes. Risk classes are sets of risks that 
share similar characteristics or nature and are grouped 
together to facilitate their management and analysis 
("Risk Classes"). Each class can include multiple 
specific risks, allowing risks to be grouped in a more 
structured and manageable way. Examples of Risk 
Classes are risks related to information security or 
risks related to the preparation of tenders.

Accordingly, the Map conveys a comprehensive view of 
the main classes of risk to which the Group's activities 
are subject and serves as a guide to the more detailed 
information provided by the different areas of 
responsibility.

The Map outlines, for classes of risk whose residual 
impact could be high or very high, the different controls 
and mitigation measures that are applied both at Group 
level and by the different companies comprising the 
Group. The Risk Department is responsible for the 
preparation of the Risk Map and its updates, which are 
carried out at least once a year.

The methodology used for the preparation and 
updating of the Risk Map includes:
• Risk Management, together with corporate officers

from different areas, reviews the Risk Classes based 
on internal and external data, deciding which ones to 
keep, delete or add.
• The corporate managers assess the likelihood and

impact of the Risk Classes based on the criteria of the 
Integrated Risk Management System, including their 
potential reputational impact. The assessment refers 
to the residual net risk, taking into account all control 
and mitigation measures in place.
• The Risk Department standardises the results and
draws up the Risk Map.
• For Risk Classes with high or very high impact, a Risk

Sheet is created with current and additional control 
and mitigation measures to be implemented.

The ACS Group implements mitigation and control 
strategies and measures aimed at reducing both the 
likelihood of occurrence of risks and their possible 
impact. The areas of responsibility are responsible for 
defining 
specific 
policies, 
overseeing 
their 
implementation and establishing acceptable levels of 
risk where necessary. These areas work continuously 
on the analysis and improvement of control and 
prevention mechanisms, ensuring the resilience and 
continuity of the Group's operations in the face of 
possible contingencies. Thanks to these measures, the 
ACS Group is better prepared to face its risks and 
protect its interests, as well as those of its 
stakeholders.

The assessment of risks in the ACS Group's risk map is 
carried out as follows: the likelihood of a given risk 
materialising is assessed on a scale of 4 levels of 
residual likelihood (very unlikely, unlikely, probable, very 
likely), once the mitigation measures have been 
considered. For its part, the residual impact is 
assessed on a scale of 5 levels (very low, low, medium, 
high, very high), which are defined based on the impact 
on the net benefit. The potential reputational risk 
associated with each risk is also assessed. This way of 
assessing risk exposure is in line with that used to 
identify 
and 
assess 
the 
risks 
associated 
with 
environmental, social and governance (ESG) factors, as 
part of the double materiality assessment and which is 
included in the Sustainability Report.

For example, in the case of the risk associated with 
tendering processes, this has been considered an 
unlikely risk in view of all the policies and measures in 
place at company and Group level, which ensure a 
thorough review of all risks associated with the project 
and the definition of all the measures necessary for 
their mitigation and control. From the viewpoint of the 
potential residual impact it is considered high since, 
although such policies mitigate the potential residual 
impact to some extent, their main effect is to reduce 
the likelihood.

Another example is the environmental and circular 
economy risk, the residual likelihood of which is 
considered unlikely because the Group, through its 
sustainability 
department 
and 
other 
areas 
of 
responsibility, ensures that projects implement all 
necessary mitigation measures to avoid environmental 
risks. However, in the unlikely event that this risk 
materialises, the residual impact could potentially be 
high.

Examples of the assessment of other risks can be 
found in the sustainability report with reference to 
climate change risks or the entire double materiality 
assessment, in both cases based on the methodologies 
set out there.

Zero Emissions for 2050 (NZE), and the time horizons 
(2022-2035) and (2035-2050) are used. Similar 
reasoning applies to this selection as for physical risks.

It has been concluded that, in terms of physical risk, the 
Group has no material net risk in the short, medium or 
long term. This is because the gross material risks due 
to possible extreme weather events in some of the 
geographical areas in which the Group operates are 
mitigated by specific measures implemented in all the 
projects that require them. These measures notably 
include: transferring risk to customers, specific 
insurance, contractual clauses covering climate risk, 
specific health and safety measures, using early 
warning systems and physical measures against 
climate impacts, among others, many of which are 
specified in the contract. In addition, the analysis has 
led to the conclusion that the company is equipped 
with the necessary adaptive capacity to cope with the 
possible evolution of hazards related to long-term 
physical risks, even for high emission scenarios.

In transition risk, the results indicate some risks 
associated with policy and law, market or reputation 
that acquire different levels of intensity depending on 
time horizons and scenarios. However, the ACS Group 
currently has a variety of measures (decarbonisation 
plans with a wide range of levers and measures, close 
monitoring of regulation, capacity for fast adaptation to 
the new regulation or anticipation of changes in the 
market) which enable it to face the possible risks of 
transition with guarantees in all the scenarios and time 
horizons analysed.

In addition, the ACS Group has identified major 
opportunities associated with the transition. Many of 
them are already an important part of the business and 
others are being developed as new markets. The ACS 
Group's vision for 2030 of being the world leader in the 
critical infrastructure of the present and the future is 
based on business lines associated with technology for 
digital transformation, energy transition, sustainable 
mobility and critical minerals; in other words, sectors 
closely linked to climate change and with growing 
demand that are expected to double the value of the 
group by 2030.

The 
integration 
of 
climate 
change 
risks 
and 
opportunities into the Comprehensive Risk Control and 
Management System and the Group's governance has 
been used to conduct a Group climate change 
resilience analysis, feeding into the Group's policies, 
strategies, actions and objectives.

| 5.8. Emerging risks management

The most significant emerging risks identified by the 
ACS Group are detailed below. They have all currently 
materialised, but it is considered that their impact on

the development of the Group's activities will be 
significantly extended over the long term (> 5 years)

Deglobalisation/
geopolitical 
conflicts: 
Disruption of the 
supply chain and 
shortage of 
construction 
materials.

Conflicts in key regions continue to affect 
global supply chains. For example, tensions 
in Eastern Europe, the Middle East and Asia 
have caused significant disruptions. 
Meanwhile, the long-lasting effects of the 
COVID-19 pandemic and tensions between 
major economic powers have exacerbated 
the energy crisis and increased commodity 
prices. There is a growing trend towards 
deglobalisation, with countries and 
economic blocs prioritising domestic 
production and supply to reduce their 
vulnerability to global disruptions.

The construction sector is very exposed to 
the difficulty of sourcing construction 
materials. This, together with the increase in 
raw material costs, the price of energy, the 
effects of climate change and the increase 
in preferences and demands of 
stakeholders, poses an increasingly 
significant risk in the market.
Inadequate use or waste of the natural 
resources necessary to carry out activities 
that do not contribute to a circular economy 
model can lead to scarcity and depletion of 
resources. 
Lastly, high demand and a future 
requirement for the use of specific 
materials with sustainable characteristics is 
expected, and it is therefore expected that 
the Group will again face supply risks from 
this type of materials over the coming 
years.

The disruption of the 
supply chain and the 
scarcity of construction 
materials directly affect 
the development of ACS 
activities around the 
world, which may involve 
delays in the 
development of projects, 
breaches of customer 
demands, failure to 
comply with the ACS 
Environmental Policy, as 
well as reputational risks, 
non-compliance with 
regulations and 
increased production 
costs.

The incorporation of circular concepts into 
the production model makes it possible to 
reduce the intensive use of natural 
resources and the high pressure on the 
environment., as well as ACS dependencies.

Resource optimisation also increases 
operational and financial efficiency, in 
addition to reducing the waste generated.

In addition, the Environmental Policy and 
the Group's Sustainability Master Plan 
define the commitments to encourage the 
use of recycled construction materials, their 
durability and efficient waste management.

The objectives of the Sustainability Master 
Plan included:
• Promoting life cycle analysis in

infrastructure and building projects, 
exceeding 200 analysed projects by 2025.
• Maintaining a waste recycling rate in
excess of 80%.
• ACS Group companies participate in

various R&D projects related to durability 
and efficiency in the use of construction 
resources and materials.

Shortage of 
available water

Water is a key resource for both building 
and social wellbeing. Overexploitation and 
mismanagement of water resources could 
lead to water stress and water scarcity.

The water shortage trends observed in 
Europe, North America and Asia, where the 
ACS Group operates, could lead to a 
decrease in water availability, both in terms 
of quantity and quality.
• Delays in construction

projects due to limited 
water availability
• Decreased demand for

construction projects 
due to rising water and 
operational costs
• Reputational damage

to the construction 
sector

The main mitigation measures are 
described in the ACS Group's Environmental 
Policy and Sustainability Master Plan and 
include, among others:
• Considering and assessing the main risks

and impacts in relation to using water 
resources.
• Identifying business opportunities to

contribute to the efficient use of water 
resources, reduce water stress and 
reduce effluent emissions.
• Promoting water consumption reduction

initiatives and water recycling/reuse 
procedures.
• Controlling the water extracted from

water-stressed areas to minimise its 
share
• Establishing a method for calculating the
water footprint

Biodiversity loss 
and ecosystem 
degradation

Biodiversity brings value to the economy 
and society by providing natural resources 
and capital and ecosystem services. 
Biodiversity decline and ecosystem 
degradation can lead to physical, 
transitional, legal, regulatory and systemic 
risks for companies.

The construction sector may be exposed to 
biodiversity transition risks, as its business 
activities affect the ecosystem and are 
therefore a potential target for future 
regulation.
• New policies and

regulations to protect 
biodiversity require 
increased enforcement 
efforts.
• Changes in consumer

preferences and 
increased attention 
from regulators could 
negatively affect the 
construction sector's 
order book.
• Reputational risks in
construction activities

The main mitigation measures are 
described in the ACS Group's Environmental 
Policy and Sustainability Master Plan.

In collaboration with customers, the public 
administration and other external 
stakeholders, the ACS Group promotes 
implementing measures to identify and 
minimise the impact on biodiversity.  These 
measures may include physical protection, 
transplantation or relocation, respect for the 
life cycles of affected plant and animal 
species, as well as offsetting the impacts of 
activities on biodiversity and forest cover 
through restoration, recovery and 
reforestation actions.

Chapter 4 of the Statement of Non-Financial 
Information and Sustainability Information 
outlines the progress of the ACS Group 
during 2024 in terms of resilience analysis 
in relation to biodiversity and the pilot 
project that has focused on the direct 
activities and part of the value chain of the 
construction sector of one of the Group's 
companies, Dragados.

cohesion

Technological 
risks and 
cybersecurity

Existence of cyberthreats that could lead to 
a prolonged shutdown of operations, 
uncontrolled access, information and data 
leaks and administrative sanctions by 
public bodies.

Digital transformation and the advance of 
new technologies have placed companies in 
a new scenario of vulnerability. At this time, 
the top priority is to work to prevent the 
vulnerability of systems to cyber-attacks 
that could have consequences that include:
• Leakage or theft of confidential or

sensitive information due to the failure or 
non-existence of information security 
systems.
• Shutdown of operating systems due to

intrusion into the systems by a third party. 
• Payment of ransoms for significant

amounts or administrative sanctions from 
public bodies (such as the AEPD).
The rapid development of AI and robotic 
systems demands their implementation in 
the Group's operations, which brings with it 
challenges to be solved to remain in a 
leading position in the market.
• Inability or difficulty in

identifying or detecting 
possible system 
vulnerabilities that may 
give rise to 
cybersecurity threats or 
attacks.
• Having a system with

insufficient resources 
to prevent and mitigate 
the IT systems’ 
vulnerabilities.
• Absence of a

formalised response 
plan that makes it 
possible to act 
efficiently in the event 
of any attack or threat.
• Lack of staff training to

introduce technological 
advances in the 
Group's operations.
• A formal security plan is in place to

identify the main IT systems risks, to be 
able to define the necessary control 
activities or projects for their mitigation in 
the short, medium and long term. 
• A person responsible for defining,

implementing and monitoring 
cybersecurity and IT security measures 
has been appointed. This person is 
independent of any organisational area, 
reporting to the management body or, 
failing that, to the Audit Committee.
• A Security Committee is in charge of

supervising the company's information 
security and approving the necessary 
resources for the correct maintenance 
and operation of the system, including the 
possibility of investing in technological 
solutions that allow for its improvement. 
• The Company works with external

companies to properly supervise and 
maintain the systems
• Joint cybersecurity projects are carried

out with other Group companies.
• Training activities are provided at least

annually to all members of the 
organisation
• A continuous system monitoring service

is available to proactively determine any 
threat that could compromise the 
company's IT systems, and vulnerabilities 
are regularly assessed through automatic 
tools or pentesting.
• Incident management and disaster

recovery procedures are defined to 
respond to and recover systems in the 
case of events that may adversely affect 
operations or infrastructure.
• Within the half-yearly reporting model of

the Global Compliance Report, which is 
homogeneous for the entire Group, the 
level of compliance with respect to 
cybersecurity and IT security is collected 
and analysed.
